coredata.dc:title: Surface effect of cesium and graphene quantum dots doped CaO to enhance catalytic dye degradation and bacterial inactivation with in-silico analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rahman, Abdul
Imran, Muhammad
Haider, Ali
Shahzadi, Anum
Ul-Hamid, Anwar
Fouda, Ahmed M.
Nazir, Ghazanfar
Ikram, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: In this study, (2 and 4 wt.%) cesium (Cs) into a fixed amount of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped CaO quantum dots (QDs) were prepared through a co-precipitation technique. To optimize the charge transfer and decrease the electron-hole (e-/h+) pair recombination rate of CaO QDs we introduced the Cs and large surface area GQDs into CaO QDs, reducing the particle size to improve catalytic effectiveness and antimicrobial efficacy. The morphology, structural and surface analysis, elemental compositions, functional groups, and optical analysis were investigated by systematically characterizing Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs. The increased active sites based on Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs revealed remarkable catalytic decolorization of rhodamine B (RhB) dye (77.83%) in an acidic medium. The maximum inhibition zones have been measured as 4.85 mm using the agar well diffusion method. Additionally, molecular docking investigations were utilized to determine the mechanism underpinning the antimicrobial action of Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs, revealing inhibition of β-lactamase and DNA gyrase.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Calcium oxide quantum dots
Catalysis
Antimicrobial activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FTIR spectra and (c–e) SAED pattern of CaO, GQDs-CaO, and (2, 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-CaO.
(a) Electronic spectra and (b) bandgap energy plot of CaO, GQDs-CaO, and (2, 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-CaO QDs.
TEM image with size distribution of the (a) CaO, (b) GQDs-CaO, and (c & d) (2 and 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-CaO QDs.
Catalysis mechanism of Cs/GQDs doped CaO.
Catalytic degradations (%) of CaO, GQDs-CaO, and Cs (2 and 4 wt.%) doped GQDs-CaO QDs in different pH media.
Photocatalytic degradations (%) of CaO, GQDs-CaO, and (2 & 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-CaO QDs in different pH media.
Graphical illustration of bactericidal activity (a) inhibition zone and (b) %age efficacy of CaO and doped CaO QDs for MDR E. coli pathogens.
3D and 2D views of Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs with effective site residues of β-lactamase (a) DNA gyrase and (b) enzyme from E. coli.
Comparison of properties of the nanocomposites with previous research.

head.ce:title: Surface effect of cesium and graphene quantum dots doped CaO to enhance catalytic dye degradation and bacterial inactivation with in-silico analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, (2 and 4 wt.%) cesium (Cs) into a fixed amount of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped CaO quantum dots (QDs) were prepared through a co-precipitation technique. To optimize the charge transfer and decrease the electron-hole (e-/h+) pair recombination rate of CaO QDs we introduced the Cs and large surface area GQDs into CaO QDs, reducing the particle size to improve catalytic effectiveness and antimicrobial efficacy. The morphology, structural and surface analysis, elemental compositions, functional groups, and optical analysis were investigated by systematically characterizing Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs. The increased active sites based on Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs revealed remarkable catalytic decolorization of rhodamine B (RhB) dye (77.83%) in an acidic medium. The maximum inhibition zones have been measured as 4.85 mm using the agar well diffusion method. Additionally, molecular docking investigations were utilized to determine the mechanism underpinning the antimicrobial action of Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs, revealing inhibition of β-lactamase and DNA gyrase.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is the most vital ingredient to all living organisms; unfortunately, a freshwater shortage has contributed to extreme consumption and wastage in recent years [1]. The harmful dyes (MB, MO and RhB) and heavy metals (Hg, Cr, Po, Pb, etc.) [2] are widely used in several industries, mainly the textile industry, for coloring purposes. Directly discharging these dyes into the freshwater resources (80% of total exudations stem from the textile industry) causes harmful environmental effects [3]. Among these dyes, Rhodamine B (RhB) is an amphoteric organic dye used in the industrial sector for various purposes like leather, printing, and dying, and it intensively pollutes aquatic zones [4]. These waste materials result in severe diseases like cancer, oral toxicity, esophagus irritations and diarrhea [5], leading to approximately 0.3 million child deaths yearly [6]. Many conventional and advanced methods have been applied, like carbon filtering, catalysis, microbial control [5], solvent extraction, membrane separation, and advanced oxidation, to extricate these harmful water-affecting impurities [6,7]. Catalysis is the more economical, energy-yielding, and ecologically friendly of these methods. It can be easily handled, cost-effective, and recyclable to remove toxic pollutants from wastewater.

Nanomaterials (NMs) are crucial in dye degradation and water treatment due to their small size, shape, and distinctive physiochemical and chemical properties. They also have proven to be a practical field for eliminating natural pathogens, including E. coli and S. aureus by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) on their surface, resulting in cell wall damage leading to bacterial death [8]. NMs, including metal oxides (MOs) such as ZnO, TiO2, Ag2O, CaO, and CuO, have been used for dye degradation and antimicrobial activity [9]. Calcium oxide (CaO), with its low cost, non-toxic nature, and unique crystalline structure, acts as an exceptional catalyst [2] for dye degradation and antimicrobial activity. CaO has some drawbacks, including relatively low surface area and surface-active sites [10]. Several strategies have been implemented to overcome these issues to enhance the surface area and surface-active sites that increased the catalytic degradation and antibacterial activity of CaO. Incorporating metals, nonmetals, and semiconductors has been suggested to enhance surface area and active sites. A catalyst with a high specific surface area can increase the reaction rate due to an increased number of organic molecules on the catalyst's surface [11].

A two-dimensional (2D) gapless semiconductor graphene intrigued the research because of its outstanding electrical properties, large surface area (2630 m²g⁻¹), low toxicity, and high thermal conductivity (5000 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹) [12,13]. Among graphene nanostructures, thin layers of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attained particular interest [14,15] due to their characteristics like photoluminescence, narrow Eg, high solubility, good electron acceptors, and donors [16,17]. GQDs have proved efficient catalysts with high surface area, good biocompatibility, excellent photo-stability, low toxicity, and robust chemical inertness [18–22]. To introduce metal ions dopants as an effective technique to increase the charge separation and to reduce the band gap. Cesium doping element was expected to significantly affect adjusting physical properties of many materials mainly due to its large atom size [23]. As an efficient contender in catalysis, incorporating Cs has been proposed due to its narrow bandgap and strong emission characteristics [24].

The study uses a co-precipitation method to synthesize Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs. The RhB degradation and bactericidal activity of the synthesized specimens were evaluated. The efficient degradation of organic dye has been observed incorporating doping that attributed to additional active sites appearing on the nanocatalyst, revealing the significant catalytic activity. The antimicrobial activity has been determined using a computational procedure (In-Silco molecular docking analysis). In addition, several characterizations were utilized to investigate the dopant-dependent characteristics of CaO QDs.

Cesium nitrate (CsNO3, 99%), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2.H2O, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%), glucose (C6H12O6), ammonia solution (NH3, 33%) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich and utilized without any further purification.

GQDs were synthesized at the laboratory using a simple glucose pyrolysis technique, which can yield pure functionalized graphene. Glucose pyrolysis is a process in which glucose and sugar are subjected to high temperatures without oxygen. The thermal decomposition of glucose molecules, forming carbon-rich materials, such as carbon nanoparticles or carbon quantum dots, under specific conditions. Recently, this technique has gained attention for converting organic waste into valuable advanced products [25]. For the synthesis of GQDs, incubation of 4 g glucose was at 260 °C to attain liquidized glucose within 20 min, and the color turned from yellow to orange. Later, the Ammonia odour was removed by stirring at 70 °C for 3 h, along with the drop-wise amalgamation of NH3 solution (12.5%) under continuous stirring. HCl was introduced for ph∼7 (neutral). Finally, residues were dried at 150 °C overnight and crushed to obtain a fine powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CaO and Cs/GQDs doped CaO
ce:section.ce:para: CaO was synthesized by using 1M CaCl2.2H2O via the co-precipitation technique. The NaOH (precipitating agent) was added drop-wise to maintain the pH∼12 of the stirred solution. Subjected to heating (80 °C) and stirring for 1 h, the resultant precipitates were centrifuged at 7000 RPM for 7 min twice, desiccated for 12 h at 150 °C, and acquired fine powder. The above procedure was implemented for a fix and various concentrations of GQDs (2 wt.%) and Cs (2, 4 wt.%) doped CaO QDs, respectively, to synthesize GQDs doped CaO and (2, 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs.

The CA of CaO and Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs was performed under NaBH4 (reducing agent) to degrade the RhB dye. Firstly, 400 µL of NaBH4 was introduced into 3 mL of RhB solution, and then 400 µL of CaO and (2 and 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-doped CaO were added. The decolorization of the dye was accomplished through the reduction of RhB. Reaction absorption progress at a specific time and room temperature was assessed via a UV-Vis spectrophotometer to calculate the RhB degradation rate. In the presence of NaBH4, RhB was reduced into leuco rhodamine B (L-RhB). Percentage degradation was computed as follows: Eq. (1);
(1)
P e r c e n t a g e
d e g r a d a t i o n
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
/
C 0
×
100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identifications and isolation of multi-drug resistant E. coli

ce:section.ce:para: Bovine mastitis milk samples were collected directly from nursing cows selected for milking; samples were collected in sterile glassware. Raw milk was taken to labs after being collected at 4 °C temperature. MacConkey agar (MCA) was utilized to confirm and purify the presence of coliforms in the bovine milk samples in triplicates after initial culturing at 5% sheep blood agar, and petri dishes were incubated for 48 h at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and recognition of bacterial isolates
ce:section.ce:para: The qualitative confirmation of E. coli was based on morphological colonies with different biochemical and Gram stain assays from Bergeys Manual of Determinative Microbiology [26].

The agar well diffusion experiment was used to determine in vitro bactericidal potential of CaO, GQDs-CaO, and Cs (2, 4 wt.%) doped GQDs-CaO against MDR E. coli isolates by analyzing the zones of inhibitions. The MacConkey agar plates were swabbed using MDR E. coli bacteria at a turbidity level of 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL (0.5 McFarland standard). The 6 mm diameter holes in the agar plates were created with sterile cork borers. Ciprofloxacin (0.005 mg/50 µL) was the +ve control, while 50 µL of DI-water acted as the -ve control. A range of CaO and doped CaO QDs for low and high concentrations filled the holes, including (0.5 mg/50 μL) and (1.0 mg/50 μL), respectively. Overnight incubation proceeded at 37 °C temperature, and a vernier caliper was used to measure the width of the inhibited zones around the wells in the petri dishes. The inhibited zone areas (mm) were examined by one-way variance analysis (ANOVA) using SPSS 23.0 software to determine the synthesized nanocomposites' antibacterial activity [26].

An appealing target for antibiotic research, cell wall biosynthesis has been hypothesized as a antibacterial mechanism of certain nanostructures. Enzymes involved in the production of peptidoglycans are essential for discovering novel antibacterial therapeutics, as their inhibition leads to the disintegration of the cell wall and, ultimately, the mortality of bacteria [27,28]. DNA gyrase and other enzymes involved in nucleic acid production also play an important role in drug discovery [29]. Here, we assessed the ability of Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs to inhibit β-lactamase E. coli and DNA gyrase E. coli enzymes. β-lactamase E. coli and DNA gyrase E. coli crystal structures were acquire from RCSB PDB (www.rcsb.org) using PDB identifiers 4KZB (Resolution: 1.72 Å) [30] and 5MMO (Resolution: 1.80 Å) [31].

To construct 3D structures and analyze QDs interactions with residues at the active site of assigned proteins, we used the SYBYL-X 2.0 tool, which we have previously reported in our investigations [32,33].
In this study, we reported the synthesis of (2, 4 wt.%) Cs into a fixed amount of GQDs doped CaO QDs through the co-precipitation method (Fig. 1 ).

XRD analysis was employed to determine phase identification and crystal structure of CaO and Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs (Fig. 2 a). The pattern shows that CaO has a cubic structure with the Fm3m space group, CaO2 has a tetragonal structure with the I4/mmm space group. In contrast, CaCO3 has a rhombohedral structure with the R3c space group. The characteristics peaks of CaO exhibited 2θ values at 26.2°, 31.5°, 39.5°, 47.5°, and 56.6°, indexing to diffraction planes (210), (114), (200), (202), and (410), respectively (JCPDS Card No.00-017-0912 and JCPDS File No. 37-1497) [34]. CaO2 exhibited the 2θ values at 22.9°, 35.5°, and 43.0° indexing to the tetragonal structure of planes (101), (110), and (211), respectively (JCPDS Card Nos. 01-085-0514 and 00-003-0865). However, it exhibited few additional noticeable peaks at 29.3° , 45.4° , and 48.5° corresponding to the CaCO3 (JCPDS Card Nos. 083-1923 and 00-047-1743), carbonation is due to the carbon dioxide in the atmosphere [35]. The Rietveld refinement of CaO was revealed in supporting information (Fig. S1). Upon GQDs doping, a small peak of GQDs at 25.2° corresponding to the (002) plane was observed [36]. Cs doping slightly increased diffraction peak intensity, which could be attributed to disturbances or defects in the structure of CaO QDs affected by cesium ion (Cs+) [37]. Peaks broadened with the addition of dopants GQDs and increased the ion concentration (Cs+), which could be attributed to lattice strain in the samples [38]. The Debye-Scherrer formula was utilized to estimate the approximate crystallite size of CaO and (2 & 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-CaO are 45.16, 46.24, 48.13, and 49.20 nm, respectively. Therefore, the size of crystallites gradually increases as the concentration of dopants rises. Moreover, as the doping concentration increased, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) decreased, and the crystal size increased [39].

To investigate the functional group and chemical bond formation of pure and Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs FTIR spectra were utilized (Fig. 2b). Transmittance bands at 3402 and 1654 cm-1 correspond to the stretching and bending vibrational modes of the hydroxyl group (–OH), respectively [6,40,41]. Bands at 1393 and 867 cm−1 corresponding to the C–O bond correlate with the carbonation of CaO nanocomposite [42], and carbonation is attributed to carbon dioxide in the atmosphere [43]. The band at 714 cm-1 specifies the Ca–O bonds, validating the production of CaO QDs [34]. Upon incorporation of GQDs and Cs in CaO, peak intensity and broadness reduction were attributed to the dopant wrapped around QDs [44]. In addition, the SAED pattern of CaO and Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs exhibited bright rings attributed to the polycrystalline nature of synthesized QDs and the rings well matched with the different XRD facets as shown in Fig. 2(c–f).

UV-Vis spectroscopy focuses on the interaction of electromagnetic radiation (such as light) with matter to study the structure of atoms, molecules, and solids with energy levels, transitions, and electronic properties [45]. UV-Vis spectroscopy was used to investigate electronic transition and information evaluating their band shifting and band structure of Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs can be indicated in Fig. 3 a. UV-Vis spectra show CaO absorbance band observed in the range of 265–315 nm, with a prominent absorption edge at 285 nm, [46] designated to transfer of an electron from Ca 4s to the 2p orbit of O, resulting in the formation of Ca2+ and O2− ions and possibly the π–π* transition [34]. The quantum confinement effect could be attributed to the addition of Cs, and GQDs shifted the CaO absorption band toward longer wavelengths (bathochromic shift), as evidenced by morphologic features containing multiple active sub-levels. The band gap energy (Eg) of QDs was measured via the Tauc plot Eq. (2).
(2)
(
α h ν
)
n
=
A
(
h ν −
E g
)

The Eg value was calculated by Eq. (2) where n = 2 as 4.35, 4.25, 4.16, and 4.05 eV for CaO and Cs-doped (2, 4 wt.%) GQDs-CaO QDs (Fig. 3b) [47]. Upon incorporating dopants in CaO QDs, the reduces Eg by modifying the width of the CB and VB regions, resulting in new energy levels within the band gap. Moreover, dopants (Cs and GQDs) occupying the substitutional position exhibited larger grain sizes, lesser scattering effects, more deformed tetragonal structure, and Eg at minimum [48,49].

TEM images investigate the structural and morphology analysis of CaO and Cs/GQDs-doped CaO, as shown in Fig. 4 (a–d). The TEM image of the CaO shows the agglomeration of non-uniform QDs (particle size = 4.5 nm), and the overlap of layered structure with QDs causes the increase of nucleation. Upon incorporating GQDs in CaO, formed a network of nanofibers that may help to charge transformation to enhance the catalytic activity. Adding Cs created a network of agglomerated QDs spherical in nature and non-uniformly distributed (Fig. 4c & d). The agglomerated QDs have a high surface area, as provided in Fig. 4a–d. The TEM images of the Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs show relatively uniform diameters (2–10 nm) with an average of 4.3 nm.

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) microscopy was utilized to find the lattice spacing of CaO and Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs (Fig. S2a-d). The interlayer d-spacing of pristine and (2, 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs were measured as 0.34, 0.35, 0.39, and 0.42 nm, respectively, and collaborated with XRD results.

EDS spectral investigations were performed to evaluate the chemical configurations and weight percentage of various elements containing pure and Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs (Fig. S3a-d). The presence of Ca and O peaks verified the synthesis of CaO. Cs peaks in the spectra (Fig. S3b-d) indicate that Cs was successfully doped into the CaO nanocomposites. The Cl peaks were detected using a precursor during synthesis, while the Au peaks were attributed to the coating applied to the samples to reduce charge effects. As shown in Fig. S3a-d, the corresponding EDS elemental mapping images of Ca, O, and Cs demonstrate the successful formation of the Cs/GQDs doped CaO nanocomposite.

To explore the different electronic asserts, chemical setup, and endorsement of the presence of Cs, GQDs, and CaO species in the produced QDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized, as illustrated in Fig. S5(a-c). The C 1s spectra of GQDs in Fig. S5 (a) exhibit distinct spikes that might be attributed to different functional categories, namely CC (284.6 eV), inoperative CC (285 eV), CN (285.9 eV), and C–N (287.9 eV) [50]. In Fig. S5(b), the Cs 3d spectra reveal two distinct spikes positioned at 724.03 eV and 738.21 eV, which could be attributed to Cs 3d5/2 and Cs 3d3/2, accordingly, as indicated in reference [51]. Two distinct spikes were seen at 346.5 and 351.2 eV energy levels, correlating to Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 orbitals in Ca2p as presented in Fig. S5(c) [52].

The catalytic redox reaction involves NaBH4 as the electron-donating species and RhB as the oxidizing species. The catalytic decolorization of RhB occurred when NaBH4 was introduced, which was a slow process. The introduction of pristine and doped CaO into RhB led to a reduction in the activation energy, which increased the reaction rate. Pure and Cs/GQDs doped CaO QDs work as electron relays, which make it easier for electrons to move toward RhB. The electron was adsorbed by the RhB surface and decolorized into LRhB (Fig. 5 ).

Catalytic dye degradation of pure and Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs was examined via UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Before adding NaBH4, the pH was maintained at 4 for acidic and 12 for basic RhB by adding H2SO4 and NaOH, respectively. The CaO and (2 and 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs acidic medium degradation (pH=4) 57.86, 77.83, 68.86 and 70.76%, in basic medium (pH=12) 46.87, 69.28, 72.2 and 75.76%, and in neutral medium (pH=7) 63.77, 71.12, 74.66 and 76.44% in 10 min, respectively. The synergistic interaction between dopants and CaO was attributed to the considerable degradation of dye in a different media (Fig. 6 ).

The pH affects the surface charge of catalysts and dye molecules, an essential factor in the catalytic process. At pH level 4, indicating an acidic environment, the surface of the CaO QDs will acquire a positive charge. In that case, the dye will have a negative charge as a result of the ionization of the carboxyl group within RhB. This may enhance the degradation of RhB dye in an acidic medium [53]. Incorporating GQDs in a pristine sample enhanced catalytic activity as GQDs prevent particle aggregation in the aqueous phase and thus improve the effective surface area and reactive sites for better catalytic reaction [54]. Upon introducing Cs/GQDs dopants, size reductions of QDs was observed, increasing the surface-to-volume ratio, thus significantly enhancing the catalytic activity [55]. Incorporating Cs alters the electronic properties of synthesized materials, such as the band gap energy and promoting charge recombination process in acidic medium, which collectively contribute to the decrease in the performance of dye degradation. Catalytic degradation of dye in acidic medium upon incorporation of Cs may prevent the reactants from diffusing to active sites, leading to decreased in the %age degradation of dye [56]. A comparison of synthesized samples with previous research is illustrated in Table 1 .

In comparisons of catalysis, photocatalytic activity (PCA) statistical calculations were carried out after visible light irradiation of samples, which led to the degradation of RhB (Fig. 7 ). Under acidic conditions (pH∼4), the degradation percentages were calculated as 49.41, 65.71, 67.28, and 68% for pure and dopant-free and doped CaO, respectively. Remediation of RhB in basic and neutral media was computed as 56.14, 50.57, 48.14, 49.71%, and 51, 49.55, 43.44, 44.44%, respectively, for CaO and (2 and 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs (Fig. 7). Results demonstrated that Cs (2 and 4 wt.%) doped GQDs-CaO QDs catalysis has, much better degradation efficiencies than PCA.

The agar well diffusion technique investigated the antimicrobial activity of doped-free and Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs against MDR E. coli isolates (Table 2 ). At low concentrations (1.90–3.35 mm) and high concentrations (2.26–4.85 mm), pure and doped CaO QDs inhibition zones were detected. Inhibitory zone measurements for MDR E. coli were compared to those of a -ve control (DI water; 0 mm) and +ve control (ciprofloxacin; 5.15 mm) (Fig. 8 a-b). Table 3 represents the comparison of the antibacterial efficacy of the nanocomposites.

The % efficacy measured against MDR E. coli bacteria increased (37 to 66%) at low and (43 to 95%) at high concentrations, respectively (Fig. 8b). By the addition of GQDs inhibition zones increased as a result of the generation of reactive sites and reactive oxygen species (ROS). The ROS produced by GQDs can harm the bacterial cell membrane, DNA, and other cellular components, ultimately causing cell death [7,61]. Studies have shown that GQDs can disrupt the bacterial cell membrane, causing internal organelles to leak out and cell death to ensue [62].

In addition, Cs doping enhanced bactericidal activity because Cs positively influences metal oxide particle formation, leading to reduced particle sizes and enhanced contact with CaO QDs and bacterial cells. Findings showed that Cs/GQDs doped-CaO nanocomposites exhibited more resilient bactericidal activity against MRD E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) with more complex structures and denser cell walls containing phospholipid bilayers. The increased production of ROS by small QDs causes membrane destruction in microorganisms, which ultimately promotes bacterial mortality via cytoplasmic organelles [61]. Anionic membrane elements of microorganisms interact with adequate Ca+2, which is accountable for increased efficiency in combating microorganisms (Fig. S6) [63].

The synthesized CaO and Cs/GQDs doped-CaO nanocomposites produce ROS when exposed to light while incubating, as represented in (Fig. S5). The prepared nanomaterial's size, shape, and surface area have assessed oxidative stress resistance [64,65]. The pairs of electrons and holes are the fundamental units responsible for producing ROS. The chemical procedure generates ROS, including the *O₂⁻, *OH, and *H₂O₂. Numerous transition metal oxides exhibit activity against microorganisms by interaction between hydrophobic and electrostatic forces. The generation of ROS can be elucidated as follows [66–68].
(3) CaO + hν → h+ + e–
(4) e– + O2 → *O2 –
(5) h+ + H2O → OH– + H+
(6) *O2 – + H+ → *HO2
(7) *HO2 + e– + H+ → *H2O2
(8) *H2O2 + *O2 - → *OH + O2 + OH–

Pure and Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs are illuminated with light having photon energy equivalent to the bandgap energy of the semiconductor. Electrons from the valence band (VB) shift to the conduction band (CB), creating vacancies (holes) in the valence band. Surface-bound oxygen molecules (O2) captured the e– in the CB, resulting in the generation of superoxide anion radicals. Similarly, H2O react with the holes in the VB, leading to the release of hydroxyl ions and hydrogen ions. The O2 – combine with H+ ions to generate *HO2. The *HO2 interacts with e– and a H+, leading to the formation of the *H2O2. Simultaneously, *H2O2 can undergo a reaction with *O2 – to produce *OH, OH–, and singlet O2. Negatively charged radicals (such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions) are unable to permeate microorganism cell membranes, but they can harm the bacteria's external layer. While, positively charged *H2O2 can easily penetrate negatively charged bacterial cell membranes and effectively kill bacteria. Consequently, *H2O2 is exceptionally harmful to bacterial cell membranes, leading to bacterial death. Electrostatic interactions between bacterial strains and composite nanomaterials generate ROS, which attach to bacterial cell membranes. This leads to the release of cytoplasmic content and disruption of cellular structure, ultimately resulting in bacterial death [69–73]. The comparison of antibacterial activity of prepared samples with the literature is depicted in Table 3.

Over the last several decades, molecular docking predictions have gained popularity as a means of unlocking the mysteries of many biological processes. The importance of nucleic acid biosynthetic pathway and cell wall synthesis (i.e., peptidoglycan biosynthesis) in discovering antibiotics has been well documented [77,78]. Several nanostructures have been antibacterial in recent years [79–81], but their precise mechanisms of action still need to be explored. The binding score of the Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs within the binding pocket of the β-lactamase enzyme in E. coli was 3.37. Fig. 9 a depicts the binding interaction pattern with critical amino acid residues of β-lactamase, including H-bonding with Ser64, Ala318, and Asn152 and pi-alkyl interaction with Val211 and Tyr221. The molecular docking predictions of Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs against DNA gyrase E. coli revealed H-bonds with key amino acid residues such as Thr165 and Gly77 and pi alkyl interactions with Arg76 and Ile78, with a binding score of 4.28 (Fig. 9b).

Molecular docking indicated a binding affinity for Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs, suggesting that these QDs may be inhibitors of β-lactamase and DNA gyrase. This prediction should be validated by in vitro enzyme inhibition approaches.

In summary, CaO and (2 and 4 wt.%) Cs/GQDs doped CaO nanocomposites synthesised through co-precipitation routes for catalytic de-colourization and antibacterial efficacy. XRD analysis affirmed the cubic, tetragonal and rhombohedral structure of CaO, CaO2, and CaCO3, respectively, and crystallinity increased upon adding Cs and GQDs. The SAED pattern confirmed that synthesized specimens were crystalline and synchronized with XRD results. Upon Cs and GQDs were introduced, the Eg was reduced, and the electronic spectra showed a bathochromic shift. FTIR exhibited that the CaO QDs have a characteristic peak at 714 cm-1; EDS spectra affirmed the existence of dopants and CaO nanocomposites. TEM analysis confirmed the formation of CaO QDs, and added Cs and GQDs increased QDs agglomeration. TEM analysis confirmed that agglomeration of QDs has a high surface area, which provides the potential catalytic and antimicrobial activity and shows diameters of QDs (2–10 nm) with an average of 4.3 nm. From HRTEM analysis, interlayer spacing was calculated as 0.34, 0.35, 0.39 and 0.42 nm. Based on the results of the experiments, GQDs doped CaO showed significant catalytic efficacy in an acidic medium (77.83%). Compared to ciprofloxacin, the prepared QDs exhibited significant inhibition zones at low and high dosages (3.35 and 4.85 mm, respectively) against MDR E. coli bacteria isolated directly from bovine mastitis milk. Cs/GQDs-doped CaO QDs exhibit bactericidal properties, and in silico docking investigations have shown that inhibition of β-lactamase and DNA gyrase may be the underlying mechanism.

Abdul Rahman: Formal analysis, Resources, Writing – original draft. Muhammad Imran: Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ali Haider: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Anum Shahzadi: Software, Writing – original draft. Anwar Ul-Hamid: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Ahmed M. Fouda: Investigation, Validation, Visualization. Ghazanfar Nazir: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation. Muhammad Ikram: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Research Project under grant number RGP2/282/45. The authors are thankful to S. Moeen, M. Mustajab, E. Umar and ZU Abidin for support in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.105510.
